Name: spectral_jacobian_routing_inference
Title: Spectral Jacobian-Guided Adaptive Routing for Bounded-Latency Inference: A PAC-Bayes Framework for Layer-Skipping with Uncertainty-Calibrated Proxies
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that layer-skipping decisions at inference time can be guided by single-pass Jacobian spectral estimates computed from pre-collected calibration data, enabling deterministic latency-bounded routing without test-time backpropagation or parallel sampling. Specifically, the top-k spectral singular values of per-layer Jacobians serve as calibrated proxies for output perturbation risk, enabling PAC-Bayes-bounded guarantees on accuracy degradation. This setting is optimal because: (1) Jacobians capture gradient flow sensitivity without requiring gradient computation during inference, (2) spectral estimation is amortizable into wall-clock budgets via single-pass SVD on mini-batches, (3) PAC-Bayes bounds provide strict accuracy-loss certificates enforceable at deployment, and (4) no alternative framework simultaneously provides training-free calibration, deterministic routing, and theoretical guarantees without incurring parallel sampling overhead.
Related Work: Prior work on dynamic depth (e.g., early-exit networks, adaptive computation time) relies either on: (a) learned gating networks requiring supervised training, (b) heuristic thresholds on activation magnitudes without theoretical guarantees, or (c) test-time optimization via gradient descent, all incurring latency or training overhead. Recent training-free quantization proxies (EMQ, SNIP) measure layer importance via Fisher information or gradient flow but do not address latency-bounded routing or provide PAC-Bayes certificates. Spectral methods in neural network analysis (e.g., neural tangent kernel theory) characterize global optimization landscapes but do not translate to inference-time layer routing with latency guarantees. Our contribution uniquely combines: (1) Jacobian spectral estimation as a training-free, latency-aware proxy, (2) explicit PAC-Bayes bounds linking spectral properties to accuracy loss, and (3) deterministic single-pass routing with amortized overhead accounting. This is distinct from prior work because it bridges spectral stability theory with inference-time routing while maintaining strict latency budgets—a gap not addressed by existing literature.
Abstract: Inference latency is a critical bottleneck in deploying large neural networks. While layer-skipping and early-exit strategies reduce computation, existing approaches either require training-time modifications, rely on heuristic activation thresholds, or lack theoretical accuracy guarantees. We propose Spectral Jacobian-Guided Routing (SJGR), a training-free inference framework that uses per-layer Jacobian spectral estimates to guide layer-skipping decisions with PAC-Bayes-bounded accuracy guarantees. Key innovation: we compute the top-k singular values of each layer's Jacobian from a small calibration set offline, then use these spectral proxies as uncertainty-calibrated thresholds for online routing decisions. The framework is grounded in PAC-Bayes theory: we derive explicit bounds linking spectral perturbation risk to expected accuracy loss, enabling deployment-time latency budgets to be mapped to accuracy certificates. Crucially, spectral estimation is amortized into wall-clock budgets via single-pass SVD, avoiding parallel sampling and memory overhead. We validate SJGR on vision transformers and language models, demonstrating: (1) latency-accuracy Pareto frontiers matching or exceeding learned gating networks without training, (2) empirical accuracy loss within PAC-Bayes bounds across distribution shifts, and (3) sub-millisecond routing overhead on single-GPU inference. Our method is modular, enabling tier-adaptive deployment: micro-grant setups use lightweight spectral caching; high-compute environments leverage full covariance offline. We provide deterministic, reproducible telemetry including perturbation-cost accounting, multi-seed variance, and exact hardware specifications.

Experiments.name: Experiment 1: Spectral Proxy Calibration and Correlation Analysis
Experiments.description: On a calibration set (500–1000 random samples), compute per-layer Jacobians via single forward-backward pass. Extract top-k singular values (k=1,5,10) for each layer. Measure correlation between spectral proxies (σ_1, σ_5, σ_10, trace(J^T J)) and per-layer output perturbation magnitude when layer is skipped (measured as ||h_l - h_l'||_2 where h_l is output with layer, h_l' is output without). Report Spearman/Pearson correlation coefficients and compute calibration curves (binned prediction vs. empirical perturbation). Metric: Correlation ρ > 0.85 for σ_1; monotonicity of σ_k vs. perturbation across all layers.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For each layer l ∈ {1,...,L}: (1) Compute J_l = ∂h_l/∂h_{l-1} via autograd on calibration batch (size 32), (2) SVD(J_l) → singular values σ_1 ≥ ... ≥ σ_d, (3) Compute perturbation δ_l = ||forward(skip_l) - forward(keep_l)||_2 on held-out test samples, (4) Regress δ_l against [σ_1, σ_5, σ_10, trace(J_l^T J_l), ||J_l||_F]. Store regression coefficients α for online routing.

Experiments.name: Experiment 2: PAC-Bayes Bound Derivation and Empirical Validation
Experiments.description: Derive PAC-Bayes bound: for any layer subset S (skip set), accuracy loss Δacc ≤ Σ_{l∈S} c_l σ_1(J_l) + √(KL(P||Q) / 2m) where c_l are empirically calibrated layer coefficients, KL is divergence between prior and posterior over skip decisions, m is calibration set size. For each latency budget B (ms), compute optimal skip set S* via dynamic programming: minimize Σ_{l∈S} latency_l subject to Δacc ≤ ε. Empirically evaluate: (1) run inference on test set with routing decisions from S*, (2) measure actual accuracy loss Δacc_empirical, (3) verify Δacc_empirical ≤ PAC-Bayes bound across 5 random seeds and 3 distribution shifts (CIFAR-10 → CIFAR-100, ImageNet → ImageNet-C). Metrics: (a) Fraction of test runs where bound holds (target: ≥95%), (b) Tightness ratio (Δacc_empirical / bound), (c) Bound slack quantification.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Dynamic programming routing: State = (layer_idx, accumulated_latency). For each layer, decide skip/keep by comparing: (skip_cost = latency_saved) vs. (accuracy_cost = c_l σ_1(J_l)). Solve via backward recursion: V[l, t] = min(skip: V[l+1, t - latency_l] + c_l σ_1(J_l), keep: V[l+1, t]). Extract optimal path. Implement single-pass routing: at inference time, for each layer l, if accumulated_latency + latency_l > B - margin, route through skip gate; else keep. No parallel sampling.

Experiments.name: Experiment 3: Latency and Overhead Accounting
Experiments.description: Profile wall-clock latency on single GPU (V100/A100) for: (1) full forward pass (baseline), (2) forward + spectral routing overhead (SVD amortization, threshold comparison). Break down overhead: (a) spectral estimation time (offline, one-time), (b) per-inference routing decision time (threshold lookup + layer skip logic), (c) memory bandwidth for Jacobian storage. Measure on ImageNet-scale ViT-Base and GPT-2 (124M). Report: (i) spectral estimation cost as % of training budget (amortized over 10k inferences), (ii) per-inference routing overhead in microseconds, (iii) memory overhead (Jacobian storage), (iv) end-to-end latency reduction (full model vs. SJGR at target accuracy). Target: routing overhead < 50 μs per inference; spectral amortization < 1% of deployment latency budget.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Offline phase: Compute SVD(J_l) for each layer once on 500-sample batch. Store top-10 singular values per layer (~10KB per layer, negligible). Online phase: Per inference, iterate through layers; for each layer, load pre-computed σ_1(J_l), compute routing score = σ_1(J_l) × input_norm (single multiply-add), compare to threshold. Time both phases with torch.cuda.Event() (GPU timer) and measure memory via torch.cuda.memory_allocated(). Repeat 1000 inferences, report mean ± std.

Experiments.name: Experiment 4: Latency-Accuracy Pareto Frontier vs. Baselines
Experiments.description: Compare SJGR against: (a) uniform layer skipping (skip every k-th layer), (b) learned gating networks (train lightweight binary classifiers per layer, standard approach), (c) magnitude-based heuristics (skip if ||h_l||_∞ < threshold), (d) random layer dropout. For each method, sweep latency budgets (10ms, 50ms, 100ms, 200ms). Measure: (i) accuracy on ImageNet/CIFAR-10, (ii) wall-clock latency (actual inference time, not FLOPs), (iii) variance across 5 random seeds. Plot Pareto frontiers: latency vs. accuracy. Metrics: (a) SJGR achieves ≥2% accuracy gain over heuristics at matched latency, (b) SJGR within 1% of learned gating without training cost, (c) latency variance < 5% across seeds.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For learned gating baseline: train binary gate g_l(h_{l-1}) per layer on 10k labeled examples (standard supervised learning, ~1 GPU-hour). For magnitude heuristics: sweep thresholds {0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0} × mean(||h_l||_∞) and pick best on validation set. For SJGR: use routing decisions from Experiment 2. Measure latency with torch.cuda.synchronize() + wall-clock timers; run 100 inference batches (batch size 32), report mean ± std latency per sample.

Experiments.name: Experiment 5: Robustness to Distribution Shift and Perturbations
Experiments.description: Test SJGR robustness across structured perturbations and domain shifts: (1) ImageNet → ImageNet-C (corruption: blur, noise, contrast), (2) CIFAR-10 → CIFAR-10-C, (3) adversarial perturbations (ℓ∞ ball, ε=8/255), (4) input noise (Gaussian, σ=0.1). For each shift, measure: (i) accuracy loss vs. full model, (ii) actual accuracy loss vs. PAC-Bayes bound, (iii) routing stability (do skip decisions remain consistent across perturbations?). Metrics: (a) PAC-Bayes bound holds under shift (empirical loss ≤ bound) for ≥90% of test samples, (b) routing stability: Hamming distance between skip sets under original vs. perturbed input < 20% of layers, (c) accuracy degradation under shift < 2× PAC-Bayes predicted loss.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For each perturbation type, generate 1000 perturbed test samples. Run SJGR routing on both original and perturbed inputs; record skip decisions and accuracy. Compute: Hamming distance of skip decisions, empirical accuracy loss, compare to PAC-Bayes bound computed on unperturbed calibration set. Compute stability radius: largest perturbation magnitude ε such that routing decisions remain stable (Hamming distance < threshold) on ≥95% of samples.

Experiments.name: Experiment 6: Ablation on Spectral Proxy Choice
Experiments.description: Isolate contribution of spectral proxies by comparing routing performance using: (a) σ_1 only, (b) top-k singular values (k=5,10), (c) Frobenius norm ||J_l||_F, (d) trace(J_l^T J_l), (e) Lipschitz constant (σ_1 + σ_2), (f) Fisher information (diagonal of J_l^T J_l). For each proxy, measure correlation with actual layer perturbation (Experiment 1) and accuracy loss under routing (Experiment 4). Report: (i) correlation coefficient, (ii) latency-accuracy Pareto frontier, (iii) PAC-Bayes bound tightness. Metric: σ_1 achieves ≥90% of best-proxy performance with minimal computation.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For each proxy p ∈ {σ_1, σ_5, σ_10, ||J||_F, trace(J^T J), σ_1+σ_2, diag(J^T J)}, use it as sole routing signal in Experiment 2 DP routing. Measure resulting accuracy loss and latency. Compute Spearman correlation between proxy value and empirical perturbation magnitude. Plot correlation vs. computational cost (time to compute proxy).

Experiments.name: Experiment 7: Scalability and Tier-Adaptive Deployment
Experiments.description: Validate modular design across compute tiers: (1) Micro-grant (single GPU, limited memory): store only σ_1 per layer + lightweight routing table; measure memory overhead and routing latency. (2) Standard lab (8-GPU node): amortize full SVD offline, store top-10 singular values, enable richer routing strategies. (3) High-compute (multi-node): compute full covariance matrix offline for joint layer-routing optimization. For each tier, measure: (i) setup time, (ii) per-inference overhead, (iii) achievable latency reduction. Metric: micro-grant tier achieves ≥80% of high-compute tier's latency-accuracy frontier with <10% memory overhead.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Tier 1 (micro-grant): Compute σ_1(J_l) for each layer via power iteration (single pass, ~100 FLOPs per layer). Store 1 float32 per layer. Tier 2 (standard): Full SVD via torch.svd(), store top-10 singular values (~40 floats per layer). Tier 3 (high-compute): Compute full Jacobian covariance matrix Σ_l = E[J_l^T J_l] offline, enable quadratic routing optimization. Measure memory and time for each tier on ViT-Base (12 layers) and GPT-2 (12 layers).

Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Spectral estimation is performed offline on small calibration sets (500–1000 samples). For large models, use power iteration (single-pass approximation) instead of full SVD, amortizing cost to <1% of deployment budget. Validate empirically in Experiment 3.

Bounds may be loose in practice, limiting practical guidance for routing. Address via: (1) empirical calibration of coefficients c_l on validation set (Experiment 2), (2) adaptive margin tuning to tighten bounds post-hoc, (3) explicit tightness quantification and reporting of bound slack.

Jacobian-based routing assumes differentiable layers. For discrete operations (e.g., attention heads, routing gates), use proxy Jacobians via straight-through estimators or substitute with layer-wise Fisher information. Document limitations and provide layer-type-specific guidance.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Distribution Shift in Calibration vs. Deployment
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Spectral proxies calibrated on one distribution may not transfer to shifted distributions. Experiment 5 explicitly tests robustness; if degradation exceeds 5%, propose online re-calibration using streaming statistics (amortized, lightweight).

Routing depends on choice of k (number of singular values), latency margin, and threshold tuning. Conduct sensitivity analysis in ablation; use validation set to tune hyperparameters once, then freeze for deployment.
Experiments focus on V100/A100 GPUs. Extend to other hardware (TPUs, mobile accelerators) if resources permit; document hardware-specific latency profiles.

Learned gating networks require training; SJGR is training-free. Ensure fair comparison by: (1) using equivalent model capacity for gating networks, (2) training on same calibration set size, (3) reporting training time and computational cost separately.

Initial experiments on ViT and GPT-2. Generalization to other architectures (CNNs, RNNs, diffusion models) requires additional validation. Frame as modular framework applicable to any differentiable architecture; provide ablation template for new domains.
